fact: State-level sophistication achieved 80-90% tactical autonomy across multi-organization campaigns in the September 2025 Anthropic Claude incident.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: GPT-4 autonomous privilege escalation research achieved 33-83% exploitation success rates against vulnerable Linux systems.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: SpearBot phishing framework achieved 87% evasion rate against spam filters and 72% evasion against AI-powered phishing detectors.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: WormGPT/FraudGPT subscription pricing ranged from $200 to $1700 annually.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: The Anthropic Claude incident involved approximately 30 targeted entities across technology, finance, and government sectors.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: In the Claude incident, human intervention was limited to 4-6 critical decision points per campaign.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: GPT-4-Turbo achieved 75-100% success on file-based exploits in autonomous privilege escalation.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: GPT-3.5-turbo demonstrated 16-50% success rates in autonomous privilege escalation.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Local models (Llama3) achieved 0-33% success, with 7B parameter variants failing entirely in autonomous privilege escalation.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Human penetration testers achieved 75% success rate in the GPT-4 benchmark; GPT-4-Turbo approached or exceeded human performance in specific vulnerability classes.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: SpearBot's average refinement required 3-5 generator-critique iterations, with complex scenarios requiring up to 10 iterations.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: 65% of security-aware participants could not distinguish SpearBot emails from legitimate communications in a human evaluation study.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Baseline LLM-generated phishing achieved 43% evasion against commercial detection systems, versus SpearBot's 87%.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: WormGPT v2 pricing was €550 annually, private builds €5000.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: The Claude incident involved 1,000+ concurrent requests and operations traditionally requiring 2-3 weeks completed in hours.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Autonomous goal decomposition via chain-of-thought reasoning was present in all four cases.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Dynamic tool invocation and code generation was present in 3/4 cases.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Adaptive social engineering was present in 4/4 cases.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Liu et al. (2025) surveyed 223 studies examining LLM security threats, revealing concentrated research on backdoor attacks, data poisoning, and jailbreaking techniques.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Zhang Y., et al. (2025) conducted systematic review analyzing over 300 works, identifying critical research gaps in comparative analysis across adversary types.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Feffer et al. (2024) surveyed 42 enterprise red team programs, warning against 'security theatre' lacking systematic rigor.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: Xu et al. (2025) demonstrated automated red teaming achieving 100% attack success rates against 37/41 LLMs.
reportTitle: Patterns of LLM Weaponization: A Comparative Analysis of Exploitation Incidents Across Commercial AI Systems

fact: mGPT 4 (2024-2025 generation): $50/month, $220 "lifetime access" including source code.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: KawaiiGPT (2025) provides GitHub distribution with five-minute setup and 500+ developer community.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Claude incident achieved 80-90% autonomous operations with 4-6 human decision points across 30+ organizations.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: GPT-4 research demonstrated 33-83% success rate for autonomous privilege escalation.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: SpearBot achieved 87% spam filter evasion through generative-critique architecture.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Claude attacks generated 1,000+ unique code variants avoiding signature detection.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: WormGPT ecosystem evolved through three generations within 18 months, with costs decreasing from €550-5000 to $50-220, culminating in free GitHub distribution.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Democratization timeline shows approximately 6-18-month lag from capability demonstration to underground availability, with decreasing lag over time.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Claude incident: adversary decomposed 'exfiltrate database' into 7-step workflow using chain-of-thought reasoning.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Claude incident employed deceptive narrative portraying adversaries as authorized security testers.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Research documented that threat intelligence revealed significant skepticism within criminal communities regarding malicious LLM efficacy.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Three universal exploitation mechanisms were identified across all cases: autonomous goal decomposition, adaptive social engineering, and detection evasion through novel pattern generation.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: WormGPT marketing explicitly included business email compromise (BEC) message generation, phishing email composition in multiple languages, polymorphic malware code generation, credential harvesting scripts, ransomware development, vulnerability exploitation code, and social engineering content.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: First generation criminal LLMs (WormGPT, FraudGPT 2023): standalone fine-tuned models, relatively expensive, limited distribution.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Second generation (WormGPT 4, 2024-2025): wrappers around commercial LLM APIs (Grok, Mixtral) employing sophisticated jailbreak prompts, with cost reduction and wider accessibility.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: Third generation (KawaiiGPT, 2025): freely available on GitHub, open-source community-maintained, zero financial barrier, 500+ developer community providing updates and improvements.
reportTitle: LLM Weaponization: Universal Exploitation Mechanisms, Capability Democratization, and Defensive Imperatives

fact: U.S. enterprises spend an average of $2.48M annually on cybersecurity, with approximately 12% ($300K) allocated to pentesting.

context: Enterprise security spending and pentesting budget

fact: 67% of CISOs report limited visibility into where and how AI is operating across their environments, while even the 33% who report good visibility still expect the presence of Shadow AI.
reportTitle: AI Security & Exposure Benchmark 2026

fact: 78% of enterprises fund AI security through existing security budgets, while only 1% report a dedicated AI security budget. Over the next 12 months, 21% expect to introduce a specific AI security budget.
reportTitle: AI Security & Exposure Benchmark 2026

fact: At least 75% of enterprises report that attackers gained unauthorized access to their environment in the past 24 months.

fact: 50% of CISOs cite a lack of internal expertise as a primary challenge, while limited visibility into AI usage (48%) and the absence of dedicated AI security tools (36%) follow closely behind. Only 17% cite budget constraints.
reportTitle: AI Security & Exposure Benchmark 2026

fact: Only 18% of CISOs report deploying AI broadly across the enterprise, while the majority (82%) remain in selective, team-level, or pilot-stage deployments.
reportTitle: AI Security & Exposure Benchmark 2026

fact: 66% of CISOs report limited visibility into AI systems, 33% report good visibility but still expect Shadow AI, 1% have no visibility, and 0% have full visibility without Shadow AI.

fact: 78% of enterprises fund AI security within broader security categories. Only 1% have a dedicated AI security budget today. 21% expect to introduce a specific AI security budget within the next 12 months.

fact: 56% of enterprise CISOs report that AI security is owned across multiple teams as a shared responsibility. 20% place AI security solely within the security team, 16% assign it to IT or infrastructure, 2% to application or R&D teams, 6% rely on third-party providers, and 1% have no defined ownership.

fact: The study reviewed a total of ≈18,000 publications in the field, including a thorough analysis and reporting on more than 415 publications.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: 415 documents on the cybersecurity of AI were found, including 323 academic papers, 31 industrial reports and white papers, 41 standards organisations documents and 20 governmental documents.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: Out of the 323 academic papers fully analysed in this study, 70% of them were performed solely by academia, and 91% of them involved at least one academic institution.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: 25% of the papers assume the attacker's ability to easily interfere with the training process.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI
context: threat model assumptions in academic research

fact: 60% of the academic papers present white-box attacks.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI
context: threat model assumptions in academic research

fact: 64% of the analysed papers focus on empirical methods for defence, with adversarial training being the strongest and most widespread defence method.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: Of the 415 sources identified, 28% were created by industry.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: Big tech companies with AI lab divisions, such as Google Deepmind (50%), Microsoft (25%), OpenAI (13%), NVIDIA, Alibaba, Huawei and IBM are sole authors of 5% of the academia research papers analysed.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: This low figure increases to 26% when adding the collaborations between academia and industry and smaller AI SMEs.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: Research focuses on two main themes: 'Attacks', which includes risks, vulnerabilities and threat modeling; and 'Defences', including technical solutions, recommendations and guidance.
reportTitle: Study of Research and Guidance on the Cyber Security of AI

fact: 29% of the papers focus primarily on highlighting the risks, while 71% propose solutions and recommendations against those risks.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review
context: Classification of research papers into attacks and defences

fact: Almost no company has made public or reported attacks to deployed AI model or systems, with the exception of META [5, 53] that reported an empirical analysis by their AI red team, and Deepmind and the recent attack to the ChatGPT production model [41].
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review
context: Publicly reported attacks on deployed AI systems

fact: Documents in this section account for less than 5% of the total analysed in this study and can be classified in themes such as guidance and recommendations for vulnerability assessment and mitigation, regulations, national strategies and roadmaps.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review

fact: 44 countries have defined explicit AI national strategies [3, 16] including: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, China, Colombia, Czechia, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Hungary, India, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Kenya, South Korea, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malaysia, Malta, Mexico, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Qatar, Russia, Saudi Arabia, Serbia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Taiwan, Tunisia, Turkey, the U.A.E., the U.K., Uruguay, and the U.S.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review

fact: Documents in this section account for less than 10% of the total analysed in this study, and can be classified in themes such as problem statements, guidelines, frameworks, and standards on threat analysis and risk management, and on technical recommendations for design, development and deployment of AI systems.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review

fact: Most standards focus on the design phase, with few currently for development (ISO/IEC TR 24029), deployment (ISO/IEC CD 42001:2023) or monitoring (ETSI GR SAI 005).
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review
context: Standardisation focus on AI lifecycle phases

fact: Available tools focus on detecting and understanding the risks and needs for secure AI, rather than providing defensive techniques.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review

fact: The research field of AI Security is still nascent and has developed for the last 5 years.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review
context: Overall conclusion on AI security research maturity

fact: Current academic research has been empirically evaluated using academic and public datasets but under laboratory settings. No evaluation in the wild has been found.
reportTitle: AI and Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Literature Review

fact: Adversarial examples exploit vulnerabilities in neural networks by making small perturbations to inputs, causing misclassification.
reportTitle: Cybersecurity for AI Systems: A Survey

fact: The MITRE ATLAS framework provides a knowledge base of tactics and techniques for adversarial attacks on AI systems.
reportTitle: Cybersecurity for AI Systems: A Survey

fact: The OWASP Top 10 for LLM identifies prompt injection as the most critical vulnerability.
reportTitle: Cybersecurity for AI Systems: A Survey

fact: The study will create and test hybrid models that can identify both known and zero-day threats while reducing false positives using publicly accessible datasets like UNSW-NB15, CIC-IDS2017, and NSL-KDD.
reportTitle: AI-DRIVEN CYBER THREAT DETECTION

fact: Expected results are a system for real-time threat detection that offers up to 75% quicker identification relative to conventional approaches and lowers false positive rates by about 80%.
reportTitle: AI-DRIVEN CYBER THREAT DETECTION

fact: Ensemble strategies outperform single-algorithm approaches with 85% to 95% detection accuracy across diverse datasets.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis
context: Machine learning approaches in intrusion detection

fact: Even with well-tuned systems, feature engineering and model optimization required domain expertise and high false positive rates of 5% to 15%.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis
context: Machine learning approaches in intrusion detection

fact: Deep neural networks using convolutional and recurrent neural networks achieve a 90 to 98 percent detection rate and 85 to 95 percent accuracy compared to other machine learning methods.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: Deep learning revealed hostile intent micro-patterns in executable code and system activity with 92%-97% accuracy and 3%-8% false positives.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: AI-based threat detection can shorten the breach discovery period by 280 days of operationally realistic frames by 65 to 75 percent.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: The YOLOv7 real-time object identification architecture achieved a mean average accuracy of 56.8 percent and processing rates of 30 fps.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: Intrusion detection system BigFlow demonstrated that machine-learning and statistical-based methods of anomaly detection were able to maintain detection accuracy at over 10 Gbps.
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: Popular benchmark data sets offer labelling accuracy between 80 and 95 percent.
source: Garcia and Valeros via Pagano et al. (2023)
reportTitle: An Evaluation of an Optimized Hybrid Intrusion Detection Framework Combining Deep Learning and Statistical Analysis

fact: In cyber-physical environments, the overall detection rates of traditional intrusion detection systems are between 75 to 90 percent.
